senses.definition: to believe that someone is capable of doing something, especially something negative or unexpected, based on their past behavior or character

examples.x: I wouldn't put anything past him, including murder.

word_list.word: [measure] other people's corn by {one's} own bushel
word_list.pronunciation: mˈɛʒɚ ˈʌðɚ pˈiːpəlz kˈɔːɹn baɪ wˈʌnz ˈoʊn bˈʊʃəl

senses.definition: to judge or evaluate others based on one's own standards, abilities, or achievements, without considering their unique circumstances or perspectives

examples.x: Despite her own financial struggles, she often measures other people's corn by her own bushel, expecting everyone to be as thrifty as she is.
Don't measure other people's corn by your own bushel; everyone has different strengths and weaknesses.
His competitive nature causes him to measure other people's corn by his own bushel, making it difficult for him to appreciate diverse approaches.
It's unfair to measure other people's corn by your own bushel; success is subjective and varies for each individual.
Instead of measuring other people's corn by his own bushel, he decided to appreciate the unique qualities and contributions each person brought to the team.

senses.definition: to meet expectations or perform satisfactorily in a given task or situation

examples.x: The new employee really cuts the mustard; she's proven to be an asset to the team.
The old computer couldn't cut the mustard for running the latest software.
He was hired as a chef because he can cut the mustard in the kitchen.
The athlete's performance in the last game didn't cut the mustard; the team expects more from him.
To be successful in this role, you need to cut the mustard and meet our high standards.

senses.definition: to succeed in reaching the desired result

examples.x: She worked hard to make the grade in her final exams and graduate with honors.
The new software didn't make the grade; it's full of bugs and needs improvement.
He was determined to make the grade and secure a spot on the national soccer team.
Our company sets high standards, and only exceptional candidates can make the grade.
To make the grade in this competitive industry, you need to continuously enhance your skills and knowledge.

senses.definition: (of a person or thing) having a flashy or showy exterior, but when examined closely or experienced fully, it fails to meet expectations or deliver on its promises

examples.x: Because of the electoral promises he made, which so far he has failed to keep, many people call the new president 'all sizzle and no steak'.
The new smartphone had all the sizzle with its sleek design and marketing, but it turned out to be all sizzle and no steak in terms of performance.
The restaurant's menu looked great, but the food was all sizzle and no steak; it lacked flavor and quality.
The candidate's campaign was all sizzle and no steak, as they failed to present concrete plans and solutions.
The flashy presentation at the conference was all sizzle and no steak; the product didn't live up to its impressive demonstration.
The new manager talked a big game about transforming the company, but after a year, it became evident that he was all sizzle and no steak
The motivational speaker was dynamic on stage, but when it came to offering practical advice, he was all sizzle and no steak, leaving the audience without substantial takeaways.

senses.definition: used of a situation, outcome, or behavior that is considered typical, expected, or unsurprising given the circumstances

examples.x: In this line of work, dealing with difficult clients is par for the course.
Traffic jams during rush hour are par for the course in a busy city.
The long wait times at the doctor's office are par for the course, unfortunately.
Experiencing a few setbacks in a complex project is par for the course.
In a competitive industry, facing rejection and challenges is par for the course on the path to success.

senses.definition: to deliver what was promised or expected, especially when it comes to achieving a particular result or fulfilling an obligation

examples.x: She was under a lot of pressure, but she always comes up with the goods when we need her most.
In the final moments of the game, the star player came up with the goods, scoring the winning goal.
Our team had to meet a tight deadline, and everyone worked tirelessly to come up with the goods.
The company's reputation for quality is built on its ability to consistently come up with the goods for its customers.
When facing a crisis, true leaders are those who can come up with the goods and make the right decisions.
